coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Qiuyan
Huang, Xiaohui
Luo, Yong
Yuan, Hanmeng
Ren, Tingting
Li, Xianjun
Xu, Dong
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: To deal with heavy metal pollution, a novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots was prepared to effectively detect and remove Cr(VI). The composition and structure of this chitosan-based hydrogel were analyzed. The performance of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel in Cr(VI) adsorption was evaluated through a contrast experiment with the normal chitosan hydrogel without carbon dots. The results showed that the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel had a higher adsorption ability of Cr(VI) (maximum adsorption capacity, 228.2 mg g−1). The effect of the initial concentration of Cr(VI) and the contact time were also evaluated. The results confirmed that the adsorption isotherm was according to the Langmuir model, and the adsorption kinetics followed the pseudo-second-order model. Furthermore, as a fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI), the detection sensitivity and selectivity were examined. The quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was achieved in the linear range of 10–80 mg L−1. Furthermore, for this fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel, the mechanism for highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI) was analyzed via FTIR and XPS. These results showed that the excellent sorption and sensing abilities of Cr(VI) may be mainly attributed to the porous structures and the additional titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots improving sorption ability of Cr(VI) and providing a rapid visual response to Cr(VI).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carbon dots
Adsorption
Detection
Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic for fabricating the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel.

Characterisations of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel. (a) SEM image; (b) optical microscopy image; (c) XRD spectra; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) UV–vis and PL spectra; (f) Excitation-dependent PL spectra; (g) Changes in fluorescence appearance of the prepared chitosan-based hydrogels.

(a) Visual description of the heavy metal sorption process; (b) Cr(VI) adsorption isotherms for FCH-6 and OCH-0; (c) kinetics of the adsorption of Cr(VI) by FCH-6 and OCH-0; (d) fitting results of the Langmuir model; (e) fitting results of the Freundlich model; (f) fitting results of the pseudo-first-order model; (g) fitting results of pseudo-second-order model; (h) comparison of fluorescent behaviors of the chitosan-based hydrogel FCH-6 after the addition of different metal ions; (i) variations in AQY of the chitosan-based hydrogel FCH-6 caused by the addition of various heavy metal ions; (j) continuous fluorescence decrease of FCH-6 with an increase in the Cr(VI) concentration.

(a) FTIR spectra before and after Cr(VI) adsorption; (b) total XPS spectra of the prepared chitosan-based hydrogel before and after Cr(VI) adsorption; (c–f) high-resolution XPS spectra of Cr2p, O1s, N1s, and Ti2p.
Cr(VI) adsorption and detection mechanism of the novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel.
Maximum sorption capacities in comparison with previous work.

row.entry: Fe3O4 nanoparticles functionalized polyvinyl Alcohol/chitosan magnetic composite hydrogel

head.ce:title: Fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots for adsorption and detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Edible Fungi Institute, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To deal with heavy metal pollution, a novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots was prepared to effectively detect and remove Cr(VI). The composition and structure of this chitosan-based hydrogel were analyzed. The performance of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel in Cr(VI) adsorption was evaluated through a contrast experiment with the normal chitosan hydrogel without carbon dots. The results showed that the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel had a higher adsorption ability of Cr(VI) (maximum adsorption capacity, 228.2 mg g−1). The effect of the initial concentration of Cr(VI) and the contact time were also evaluated. The results confirmed that the adsorption isotherm was according to the Langmuir model, and the adsorption kinetics followed the pseudo-second-order model. Furthermore, as a fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI), the detection sensitivity and selectivity were examined. The quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was achieved in the linear range of 10–80 mg L−1. Furthermore, for this fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel, the mechanism for highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI) was analyzed via FTIR and XPS. These results showed that the excellent sorption and sensing abilities of Cr(VI) may be mainly attributed to the porous structures and the additional titanate and cellulose nanofibers modified with carbon dots improving sorption ability of Cr(VI) and providing a rapid visual response to Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are widely recognized to be harmful to the environment due to their toxicity and nonbiodegradability [1–3]. Among these heavy metal ions, Cr(VI) ion, which is produced and discharged from many industrial processes, is one of the most prominent hazardous contaminants. Cr(VI) ion can enter the human body and cause many symptoms and diseases [4,5]. Hence, the design and fabrication of novel adsorbent for removing Cr(VI) ion is necessary.

Chitosan (CS) is the second largest natural polymer; it is widely distributed in nature and is usually obtained from waste biomass [6,7]. Because CS is biodegradable, nontoxic, renewable and contains many amino and hydroxyl groups, which electrostatically interact with heavy metals, it is considered as a useful material in water treatment for removing heavy metals. Zhang et al. prepared malic acid-enhanced chitosan hydrogel to remove Cr(VI) from an aqueous solution [8]. Sharma et al. fabricated chitosan-crosslinked-poly(alginic acid) nanohydrogel using the copolymerization method as an adsorbent for Cr(VI) [9]. Vilela et al. prepared a chitosan-based hydrogel for removing Cr(VI), and the maximum sorption capacity was measured to be 73.14 mg g−1 [10]. In these applications, CS is rarely used by itself because of its limited flexibility; therefore, various methods (e.g., blending, grafting, and crosslinking with other preferable nanomaterials) have been developed to enhance its performance [11,12].

Based on these developed methods, many nanomaterials can be incorporated into the matrix of the chitosan-based hydrogel [13,14]. Some nanomaterials such as TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCN), carbon dots (CD) and titanate nanofibers (TN) are expected to be used to enhance the performance of the chitosan-based hydrogel. TOCN, a type of green functional material, has many carboxyl groups, which originate mainly from the oxidation of primary hydroxyl groups [15]. Because of its inherent characteristics (e.g., biodegradability, good dispersion stability, high mechanical stiffness, ease of forming a network structure, and abundance of binding sites for heavy metals), TOCN helps to enhance the adsorption performance [16]. Meanwhile, CD has emerged as a biological and chemical sensor because of its good fluorescence property, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity [17,18]. The good fluorescence property shows considerable potential for new applications in analytical chemistry, especially in environmental sensing, such as the detection of heavy metals. Hence, the introduction of CD can help the chitosan-based hydrogel to develop new functions. Furthermore, TN, an inorganic adsorption material [19], is generally assumed to be a promising candidate for the removal of heavy metals because of its high surface area, large adsorption capacity, fast adsorption rate, and chemical stability. Because of these excellent characteristics of TOCN, CD, and TN, the motivation of this work is to use these three nanomaterials to develop a novel chitosan-based hydrogel to enhance adsorption performance and add new features.

In this study, a novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel with TOCN, CD and TN was prepared and characterized. The adsorption behaviors for Cr(VI) onto this hydrogel were examined. The equilibrium isotherms and adsorption kinetics models were evaluated. Furthermore, the fluorescent detection for Cr(VI) was studied, in which the detection sensitivity and selectivity were examined. On this basis, the mechanism of highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI) for this novel chitosan-based hydrogel was determined.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO), ethylenediamine (EDA), citric acid (CA), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), titanyl sulfate(TiOSO4), chitosan (CS), acetic acid (AA) and glutaric dialdehyde (GD) were bought from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company.

The fabrication procedure for TOCN has been previously reported [20], and we used the same procedure in this study. Initially, softwood kraft pulp (3 g) was suspended in distilled water (300 mL) and then mixed with NaBr (0.3 g), aqueous NaClO solution (20 g, 10 wt%), and TEMPO (0.05 g). The pH of the reaction solution was adjusted to approximately 11.0 by adding a 1 mol⋅L−1 NaOH solution. This oxidation reaction required 6 h and was stopped by dripping 60 mL of ethanol. Then, the mixture was washed with distilled water until a neutral pH was obtained. Then, it was passed through a high-pressure homogenizer five times. After the homogenization, TOCN was obtained and dialyzed for 4 d. The average carboxyl content of TOCN was 0.78 mmol g−1. Finally, TOCN was adjusted to 0.5 wt% by adding water.

According to the reference [21], CD was successfully synthesized using the same hydrothermal reaction. Initially, a mixture of CA (0.21 g), EDA (268 μL), and water (5 mL) was stirred for 2 h and transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessel and heated at 300 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature. Then, the solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous filter and purified using a dialysis bag in water for 4 d. Subsequently, water-soluble CD (0.1 wt%) was obtained.

TOCN-CD was prepared using the condensation reaction [22]. The TOCN suspension (20 mL, 0.5 wt%) was continuously stirred at 40 °C for 2 h. Then, an acetate buffer (5 mL, PH = 3.5), EDC (60 mg), NHS (230 mg) and CD (15 mg) were added to the TOCN suspension at 40 °C with continuous stirring. Later, the mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 12 h to form TOCN-CD. TOCN-CD was purified through a dialysis bag in water for 4 d. Subsequently, TOCN-CD (0.5 wt%) was obtained.

TN was prepared using the typical hydrothermal reaction [23]. TiOSO4 (3.57 g) was suspended in distilled water (26.66 mL) and then mixed with a NaOH solution (15 M, 66.66 mL) under ultrasonic stirring. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined pressure vessel and heated at 200 °C for 48 h to yield TN. Subsequently, TN was washed with distilled water until a neutral pH was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel was prepared using the crosslinking reaction [24]. Initially, TOCN-CD (0.5 wt%) was mixed with AA (2 wt%) at 1:1 by volume and stirred for 1 h. CS (3.0 g) was resolved in the solution of AA (147.0 g, 2 wt%) with whisking for 1 h. Additionally, TN (0.065 g) was continuously stirred in the solution of AA (15 g, 2 wt%) until it was mixed well. Subsequently, a series of different amounts of TOCN-CD AA suspension (0.01, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, and 4.0 g) were used as initial reactants. Then, the CS AA solution (16 g) and TN AA suspension (15 g) were added to the different contents of TOCN-CD AA suspensions with continuous stirring until the mixture became pellucid. Then, the GD solution (3 mL, 10 wt%) was quickly added to the abovementioned mixture. The fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel was formed at room temperature for 2 h. The formed hydrogel was soaked in water for 4 d with a frequent replenishment of water to remove any unreacted species. Finally, these prepared hydrogels were denoted as FCH-1, FCH-2, FCH-3, FCH-4, FCH-5, FCH-6, FCH-7, FCH-8, and FCH-9.

The sorption kinetics and isotherms for the chitosan-based hydrogel FCH-6 and original chitosan-based hydrogel OCH-0 were examined by batch experiments. These sorption experiments had three replications. The prepared hydrogel (10 g) and Cr(VI) ion solution (200 mL) were added into a 250 mL flask. Then, the flask was placed in a water bath shaker at the speed of 150 rpm. The sorption temperature was kept at 30 °C.

The effects of different factors were examined in these batch experiments: various Cr(VI) concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mg L−1) at the fixed contact time (2880 min) and different contact times (5, 20, 40, 80, 120, and 160 min) at the fixed initial concentration (100 mg L−1). The Cr(VI) concentration at equilibrium was determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS).

The surface morphology of these prepared hydrogels was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The crystalline structures of these prepared hydrogels were observed using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany). The functional groups of these prepared hydrogels were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet IN 10TM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra of these prepared hydrogels were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectrum was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS9800, Edinburgh Instruments, England). The elemental compositions of these hydrogels were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Sigma Probe, VG Scientific, UK). The Cr(VI) concentration in the solution was determined by ICP-MS (iCAP6300, Thermo, USA).

The schematic for fabricating the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel is shown in Fig. 1 . Compared with the original chitosan hydrogel, TOCN, CD, and TN were three additional elements. First, TEMPO-mediated oxidation and high-pressure homogenization were used to prepare TOCN, and hydrothermal reaction was applied to fabricate CD. Then, TOCN-CD was synthesized via the condensation reaction using TOCN and CD as raw materials. The crosslinking reaction of CS and GD occurred to form the novel chitosan-based hydrogel in the existence of TOCN-CD and TN which were connected with the CS network by hydrogen bonding. In the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel, TOCN-CD acted as a natural frame to facilitate the formation of porous structures, which provided many mass transfer channels; additionally, TOCN-CD acted as an optical sensor for the fluorescent detection of heavy metals. Meanwhile, TN modified the network structures of the chitosan-based hydrogel and increased its surface area for metal adsorption.

The porous structures and morphologies of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel can be clearly seen in SEM (Fig. 2 a) and optical microscopy (Fig. 2b) images. 3D interconnected porous structures with different sizes were observed in the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel. In this case, more binding sites and mass transfer channels were exposed to heavy metal ions, which enhanced adsorption capacity and adsorption rate.

TN, CS, TOCN, TONF-CD, and FCH-6 were characterized by XRD (Fig. 2c). TN exhibited eight significant dispersion peaks, which were consistent with previous reports [25]. CS exhibited a broad and less intense peak (2θ = 20.0°) which was attributed to the amorphous structure of CS [26]. Both TOCN and TOCN-CD showed three clear peaks (2θ = 22.7°, 16.2° and 14.9°), which corresponded to the crystalline structure of cellulose I [27]. For FCH-6, these main characteristic peaks developed variations. The peaks belonging to TN cannot be clearly observed, which may be due to the blending of TN into the network structure of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel, which reduces crystallinity. The peaks belonging to CS disappeared, which could be due to the crosslinking reaction of CS, which reduces the original crystal structure. Additionally, three clear peaks (2θ = 22.7°, 16.2°, and 14.9°) were not observed in the XRD spectrum of FCH-6. This may be due to the linking of TOCN-CD with CS through hydrogen bonding, which reduces the internal hydrogen bonding of the TOCN-CD chain and destroys its crystalline structure.

The functional groups of TN, CS, TOCN, TOCN-CD, and FCH-6 were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. For TN, the typical band at 909 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of Ti–O, which involves non-bridging oxygen atoms [28]. In the CS spectrum, the broad band at 3350 cm−1 belonged to the OH and NH stretching vibrations, and the band at 1550 cm−1 was related to the –NH2 bending vibration [24]. For TOCN and TOCN-CD, some typical bands of cellulose species were shown. A new band attributed to the –CO–NH– stretching vibration appeared at 1650 cm−1 for TOCN-CD, which suggested that CD was loaded on TOCN [22]. In the case of FCH-6, the abovementioned characteristic bands for CS and TOCN-CD were observed, and a new band at 1647 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of –CC–NC– was observed [29], which indicated that TOCN-CD was physically attached into the chitosan-based hydrogel and the crosslinking reaction of CS and GD occurred. Meanwhile, the typical band at 909 cm−1 from TN disappeared, which was due to the connection between TOCN-CD and CS network through hydrogen bonding.

The UV–vis spectrum of FCH-6 is shown in Fig. 2e. There was one broad, unresolved envelope in which one small peak at 344 nm originated from CD [21]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of FCH-6 are also illustrated in Fig. 2e. Optimal excitation (423 nm) and emission (526 nm) bands were observed. With an increase in the excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission band of FCH-6 moved to a higher region (Fig. 2f). This behavior was due to the surface state affecting the bandgap of CD.

Additionally, this chitosan-based hydrogel exhibited green fluorescence under UV-light irradiation (365 nm) using a handheld UV lamp. Changes in the fluorescence appearance of FCH-1, FCH-2, FCH-3, FCH-4, FCH-5, FCH-6, FCH-7, FCH-8 and FCH-9 are shown in Fig. 2g. The observed fluorescence was different, and FCH-6 showed the strongest fluorescence. The absolute quantum yield (AQY) of FCH-6 was measured to be 4.6%.

A visual description of the heavy metal sorption process is provided in Fig. 3 a. Initially, one cylinder of the prepared chitosan-based hydrogel (diameter: 3.5 cm, thickness: 3.0 cm) was put in a beaker. Then, a CuSO4 solution (1500 mg L−1, 125 mL) was added under continuous stirring for 10 min; the solution had blue color. After 2 h, the solution became clear. It was shown that the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel had highly efficient adsorption ability for heavy metals.

The Cr(VI) adsorption isotherms for FCH-6 and OCH-0 are shown in Fig. 3b. The adsorption of Cr(VI) for FCH-6 and OCH-0 increased with an increase in ion concentration. Notably, all equilibrium adsorption capacities of FCH-6 were greater than those of OCH-0. It was confirmed that the addition of TOCN-CD and TN to the chitosan-based hydrogel enhanced the adsorption ability. Furthermore, two common isotherm models (i.e., Langmuir and Freundlich models) were used to fit the experimental data of FCH-6 (Fig. 3d and 3e) [30]. The correlation coefficients (R2 ) from these two models were both above 0.99; however, R2 from the Langmuir model was greater than that from the Freundlich model. Based on the fitting results of the Langmuir model (Fig. 3d), the maximum sorption capacity of FCH-6 was computed to be 228.2 mg g−1. The maximum sorption capacities of other chitosan-based hydrogels are also summarized in Table 1 . There were clear differences between these maximum sorption capacities. It was confirmed that this novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel was a promising adsorbent.

The sorption kinetics of FCH-6 and OCH-0 are shown in Fig. 3c. For FCH-6, the adsorption of Cr(VI) ions rapidly increased during the first 100 min, and reached approximately 90% of the maximum adsorption amount; then, the Cr(VI) adsorption slowly increased. Thus, the adsorption of OCH-0 could only increase fast for the first 130 min. This result showed that the sorption speed of FCH-6 was more than that of OCH-0. Furthermore, two commonly used kinetic models (i.e., pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models) were used to analyze the kinetics data of FCH-6 (Fig. 3f and 3 g) [38]. Furthermore, the fitting results of these two models are shown in the abovementioned figures. Notably, R2 from the pseudo-second-order model was greater than that from the pseudo-first-order model. These results indicated that the applicability of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was feasible for describing the adsorption process of Cr(VI) on the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel.

Furthermore, as a solid-phase fluorescent probe, this novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel can be used to selectively and sensitively determine Cr(VI) ions. The selectivity was evaluated by testing the fluorescent response of the chitosan-based hydrogel, FCH-6 to various heavy metals (Fig. 3h and 3i). In this process, we examined the fluorescence quenching effect of various metal ions (Na+, Hg2+, Cr6+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Fe2+) at the concentration of 2000 mg L−1. Among these tested metal ions, Cr(VI) ions had the greatest effect on fluorescence quenching, which may be explained that Cr(VI) ions have the strongest electron-capturing ability; thus, more electrons are transferred from the CD to Cr(VI) ions, resulting in the strongest fluorescence quenching. The results confirmed the excellent selectivity of the chitosan-based hydrogel. The sensitivity of Cr(VI) detection was further discussed. The continuous fluorescence decrease of FCH-6 under ultraviolet lamp was observed when the Cr(VI) concentration increased (Fig. 3j). Thus, the AQY of FCH-6 reduced from 4.6% to 0% within a certain range of Cr(VI) concentration. The linear calibration was in the concentration range of 10–80 mg L−1 with a regression equation of AQY = − 0.0165 × C0 + 4.5460, where C0 is the concentration of Cr(VI) and R 2 is 0.99351. Using the 3σ rule (σ = S0/S, S0 is known as the standard deviation of the blank after repeated measurements, and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the detection limit of Cr(VI) can be calculated to be 8.5 mg L−1.

The characterization results of the prepared fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel indicated that this hydrogel exhibited a highly efficient adsorption and detection of Cr(VI). To understand the Cr(VI) adsorption and detection mechanism of the prepared fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel, FTIR and XPS spectra were used.

The FTIR spectra before and after sorption are shown in Fig. 4 a. The FTIR spectrum of FCH-6 before sorption shows the characteristic absorption band of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations at 3350 cm−1, two bands associated with –COO− stretching vibration at 1560 and 1406 cm−1 as well as the band of CO–NH stretching vibration at 1650 cm−1. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of FCH-6 after sorption, these four abovementioned bands decreased; the characteristic absorption band at 902 cm−1, which is assigned to chromium ions, increased [39]. The abovementioned changes confirmed that –NH2, –COOH, and –OH were effective for the adsorption of Cr(VI).

To study the mechanism of FCH-6, XPS analysis was conducted. The total XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows four peaks at 285, 399, 462, and 532 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, Ti2p and O1s, respectively. After the Cr(VI) adsorption, two new peaks at 579 and 589 eV appeared, which were related to Cr2p; this demonstrated that Cr(VI) ions were successfully adsorbed by the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel. The high-resolution Cr2p spectrum (Fig. 4c) showed that both Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 envelopes were divided into two specific Cr(VI) and Cr(III) peaks. The binding energies at 579.9 and 589.0 eV were related to Cr(VI). Meanwhile, two binding energies at 577.1 and 586.7 eV were associated with Cr(III). This demonstrated that many Cr(VI) ions were reduced to Cr(III) ions [40], and reduced Cr(III) ions were also attached to the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of FCH-6 (Fig. 4d) can be divided into two single peaks, corresponding to C = O (532.7 eV) and C–O (531.0 eV). After the adsorption of Cr(VI) ions, both peaks showed small movements, whereas the peak area ratio changed from 0.55 to 4.65. This change implied that there was an association between the chromium ion and oxygen atom in OH and COO groups. Meanwhile, N1s spectrum of FCH-6 before adsorption is shown in Fig. 4e. Compared with the XPS spectrum acquired after Cr(VI) adsorption, new peaks were not observed, and there was only some movement of the original peak. This result indicated that the amino group participated in Cr(VI) adsorption, and complexation did not occur [41]. Additionally, Fig. 4f shows the Ti2p spectra of FCH-6 before and after adsorption. New peaks were not observed, and there were only some movements for these two original peaks. This result confirmed that Cr(VI) ions were removed by TN via ion exchange.

Based on the abovementioned results, the Cr(VI) adsorption and detection mechanism of the novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel can be as follows (Fig. 5 ). First, because of the presence of amino groups, Cr(VI) ions were easily adsorbed on the surface of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel by electrostatic attraction. Furthermore, because the porous structures of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel served as the mass transfer channel, Cr(VI) ions were quickly moved from the surface to the inside area. Meanwhile, the Cr(VI) to Cr(III) reduction process occurred through the redox reaction of existing carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The reduced Cr(III) ions were adsorbed on the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel through ion exchange. For these unique structures of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel, a highly efficient adsorption is obtained for Cr(VI). Additionally, CD was wrapped in the porous structures of the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel as the optical sensor of Cr(VI). For the same reason of electrostatic attraction, Cr(VI) ions were quickly adsorbed on CD, which resulted in fluorescence quenching. Depending on the rapid visual response to Cr(VI), the fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel showed a highly efficient detection for Cr(VI).

A novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel was prepared by incorporating TOCN-CD and TN. Adsorption equilibrium isotherms and kinetics illustrated that adsorption in this hydrogel followed Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models, and the maximum sorption capacity for Cr(VI) ions enhanced to be 228.2 mg g−1. Furthermore, the novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel achieved the detection limit of 8.5 mg L−1 with an analytical linear range of 10–80 mg/L for the selective and sensitive determination of Cr(VI). The XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed that the addition of TOCN-CD and TN could build porous structures and mass transfer channels and provide more binding sites and a rapid visual response to Cr(VI), which resulted in the considerable improvement of adsorption and detection of Cr(VI). Overall, the novel fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel can be considered as a promising adsorbent for the removal of Cr(VI) ions because of its high adsorption capacity and ability to detect Cr(VI) ions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financed by Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71), Special Projects of Scientific and Technological Innovation in Hunan Forestry, China (Grant No. XLK201982), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 2019JJ50981), Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 18A166) and Hunan Provincial College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (Grant No. 2495).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cell surface engineering of microorganisms towards adsorption of heavy metals
Crit. Rev. Microbiol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Li, H. Tao, Cell surface engineering of microorganisms towards adsorption of heavy metals, Crit. Rev. Microbiol. 41 (2015), 140-149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on the adsorption isotherms of chromium (VI) by means of carbon nano tubes from aqueous solutions
Environ. Eng. Manag. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Najafpoor, G. Soleimani, M. Ehrampoush, M. Ghaneian, E. Rahmanpour Salmani, M. Dolatabadi, Study on the adsorption isotherms of chromium (VI) by means of carbon nano tubes from aqueous solutions, Environ. Eng. Manag. J. 1 (2014), 19-23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metal removal from water by magnetite nanorods
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Karami, Heavy metal removal from water by magnetite nanorods, Chem. Eng. J. 219 (2013), 209-216.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of toxic hexavalent chromium from aqueous solution by nickel ferrite-polyaniline nanocomposite
Desalin. Water Treat.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Agrawal, N.B. Singh, Removal of toxic hexavalent chromium from aqueous solution by nickel ferrite-polyaniline nanocomposite, Desalin. Water Treat. 57 (2016), 17757-17766.
